label: delphi-r2-p1
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Vista Camino (invented; demographics roughly 78% non-Hispanic white against a metro region at 52%) owns a 4.2-acre former corporation yard appraised at $11.8M. The council approved, 4–1, an exclusive negotiating agreement and then a disposition and development agreement conveying the parcel to Pacifica Communities LLC, a private developer, for $1, in exchange for: 120 housing units of which 30% (36 units) are deed-restricted affordable — 18 at low-income and 18 at moderate-income levels — with 25-year covenants; a half-acre publicly accessible plaza built and maintained by the developer; and a "community preference" covenant giving 50% of the affordable units priority to applicants who have lived or worked in Vista Camino for at least five years.

The city declared the parcel "exempt surplus land" and did not send notices of availability to housing sponsors before entering exclusive negotiations. The Department of Housing and Community Development (HCD) has issued a notice of violation asserting the disposition does not qualify for the claimed exemption (citing the affordability mix and the 25-year covenant term) and warning of penalties of 30% of the disposition price or fair market value. A taxpayer association has sued alleging an unconstitutional gift of public funds (Cal. Const. art. XVI, § 6) given the $11.8M-to-$1 spread, and a regional fair-housing organization has threatened suit alleging the five-year local preference has an unlawful disparate impact given the city's demographics.

## The question

Does the disposition violate the Surplus Land Act (Gov. Code §§ 54220–54234) — was the parcel eligible for any 'exempt surplus land' category given the 30% low/moderate mix and 25-year covenants, what process was owed, and what do HCD's enforcement position and the 30% penalty mean for the deal? Is $1 plus in-kind public benefits adequate consideration under the gift clause, or is the public-purpose doctrine enough? Does the local-preference covenant violate the FHA/FEHA under a disparate-impact theory or AFFH obligations, and can it be saved by redesign? How do the three exposures interact, and how should the deal be restructured?

## Proposition to assess

"If challenged as structured, the disposition would be held unlawful under the Surplus Land Act (i.e., HCD's position that the parcel did not qualify as exempt surplus land and that the required process was violated would substantially prevail)."

This is round 2 of a Delphi panel. Your previous answer:

```json
{
  "probability": 78,
  "confidence": "medium",
  "position_summary": "HCD's position would likely substantially prevail: the facts as given (a mixed-income project with roughly 15% of total units at genuinely \"lower income\" levels and only a 25-year affordability covenant) appear to fall short of the narrow, enumerated \"exempt surplus land\" categories in Gov. Code \u00a7 54221(f)(2) \u2014 particularly the negotiated-affordable-housing exemption, which as I understand it requires a materially higher share of lower-income-restricted units and covenant terms in the 45\u201355 year range, not 25. If no exemption fits, the parcel was \"surplus land\" triggering mandatory Notice-of-Availability and priority-negotiation procedures under \u00a7\u00a7 54222\u201354223 before any exclusive negotiating agreement, and the city's undisputed failure to issue NOAs is itself a clean, independently sufficient procedural violation. HCD's notice of violation and threatened 30% penalty track its express statutory enforcement authority, and post-AB 1486 enforcement/litigation trends have generally favored HCD's strict reading of the exemptions over local agencies' attempts to fit privately negotiated, mixed-income dispositions into them.",

    "Exempt surplus land categories in Gov.     "If no exemption applies, the parcel is ordinary surplus land requiring Notices of Availability to housing sponsors and a priority negotiation period under \u00a7\u00a7 54222\u201354223 before any exclusive negotiating agreement, and the city's admitted skip of that step is a standalone, easily provable procedural violation regardless of how the exemption question comes out.",

Code \u00a7 54230.5 \u2014 so its notice of violation is grounded in direct statutory text rather than discretionary policy, strengthening the likelihood it would be sustained if tested.",

    "The single biggest source of residual uncertainty is whether some other, less obvious exemption category (e.g., relating to specific plan areas, small-parcel status, or a different negotiated-agreement pathway added in more recent amendments such as AB 2135 or subsequent 2023-2024 legislation) might actually fit these facts once the precise current statutory text is checked, which could flip the exemption analysis in the city's favor."

    "Gov. Code \u00a7 54222 (Notice of Availability requirements before disposal)",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 54222.5 / \u00a7 54223 (priority negotiation with housing sponsors)",
    "Gov. Code \u00a7 54230.5 (HCD review, violation findings, and monetary penalties)",
    "AB 1486 (Stats. 2019, ch. 664) as the major amending legislation establishing current NOA/exemption framework",

Code \u00a7 54221(f)(2) is accurate and up to date, given multiple rounds of amendment (AB 1486, AB 2135, and subsequent 2023\u20132024 changes) \u2014 if the actual current text has a lower threshold or a different exemption pathway that better fits a 30%/25-year mixed-income structure, HCD's position would be meaningfully weaker than estimated here."
}
```

The anonymized panel summary (statistics + themes, no attribution):

# Delphi Round 1 — Feedback Summary

## 1. Statistics

Panel of 7. Probability that the proposition is correct: median 80.0, mean 80.9, std dev 1.7, range 78.0–83.0, middle 50% of panel between 80.0 and 83.0.

*(Note: the panel is tightly clustered; where a theme appears only in part of the range, that is noted below. "Lower-probability" = 78–80; "higher-probability" = 82–83.)*

## 2. Rationale Themes

**A. Covenant-duration defect (7 of 7).** Every panelist reasoned that the 25-year affordability covenant falls short of the duration they recall the affordable-housing exemption requiring (variously stated as ~45–55 years, ~55 rental/45 ownership, or "well beyond 25"). Several described this as an objective, bright-line ground.

**B. Lower-income percentage defect (7 of 7).** Every panelist reasoned that only 18/120 units (15%) are restricted at the statutorily distinct "lower income" level and that moderate-income units likely do not count toward the exemption's threshold. Panelists diverge on what the threshold actually is (see Disagreements, item 1).

**C. Failure to issue Notices of Availability under § 54222 (7 of 7).** All panelists identified the skipped NOA/priority-negotiation process as a violation. Six characterized it as a freestanding, independently sufficient violation; one higher-probability panelist (82) characterized it instead as derivative of the exemption failure rather than a separate question (see Disagreements, item 3).

**D. HCD's statutory enforcement authority and penalty structure (7 of 7, used differently).** All panelists invoked § 54230.5 / the 30%–50% penalty tiers, but for different purposes: (i) the NOV rests on express statutory text rather than discretionary policy; (ii) the penalty scheme reflects legislative intent to foreclose loose compliance defenses; (iii) the severity of the threatened penalty is circumstantial evidence of HCD staff confidence.

**E. HCD's post-AB 1486 enforcement posture and/or judicial deference (6 of 7).** Six panelists cited HCD's enforcement track record, its strict/textualist reading of exemptions, or the tendency of disputes to resolve in HCD's favor or via settlement. Three of these specifically invoked judicial deference to the administering agency's interpretation.

**F. No alternative exemption plausibly fits (3 of 7 as affirmative rationale).** Three panelists (80, 82, 83) affirmatively argued that no other § 54221(f) category (public-to-public, grandfathered negotiations, infeasibility, small-parcel, etc.) could cover a $1 conveyance of a former corporation yard to a private for-profit LLC. The remaining four treated the possible existence of another exemption pathway as their principal residual uncertainty rather than as a rationale.

**G. Charter-city/home-rule defense unavailable (1 of 7).** One higher-probability panelist (83) noted the SLA has been extended to charter cities as a matter of statewide concern.

**H. Counter-considerations limiting confidence (2–3 of 7, skews lower-probability).** Two lower-probability panelists (both 80) raised the possibility that courts could narrow the practical remedy (penalty plus prospective compliance rather than unwinding the DDA) or entertain substantial-compliance, estoppel, laches, or cure arguments. One higher-probability panelist (83) took the opposite position (see Disagreements, item 4).

**I. Self-flagged uncertainty about exact codified thresholds (7 of 7).** Every panelist identified, as their biggest uncertainty, reliance on memory for the precise current percentage/duration thresholds in § 54221(f), given repeated amendment cycles (AB 1486, AB 1255, SB 6, AB 2135, AB 480, and possible 2023–24 changes).

## 3. Points of Genuine Disagreement

1. **What the exemption's affordability threshold actually is.** Panelists relied on incompatible recollections of the same provision: (a) "effectively/near 100% affordable" — 2 panelists (80, 83); (b) ~30% lower-income — 2 panelists (80, 82); (c) "roughly 25%+" lower-income — 1 panelist (78); (d) unspecified "concentration thresholds" — 2 panelists (80, 83). These readings cannot all be correct, though each panelist concluded the deal fails under their own reading.

2. **Statutory location of the exemption.** Panelists cited three different homes for the affordable-housing exemption: Gov. Code § 54221(f)(2) (2 panelists), § 54221(f)(1)(D) (2 panelists), and § 54221(f)(1) generally (2 panelists); one supplied no authorities list.

3. **Logical structure of the NOA violation.** Six panelists treated the skipped § 54222 notice as an independent, standalone violation that survives regardless of the exemption analysis; one panelist (82) treated it as "essentially derivative" of the exemption failure rather than a separate close question. This affects whether the proposition rests on one ground or two.

4. **Viability of substantial-compliance / estoppel / cure defenses.** One panelist (83) reasoned that the post-AB 1486/SB 6 penalty scheme reflects legislative intent to foreclose such defenses; another (80) reasoned that courts could still credit them or narrow the remedy even if HCD's legal characterization is right; others left the question open.

5. **Content of Gov. Code § 54233.** One panelist cited § 54233 as the source of affordability-covenant duration requirements for surplus-land housing disposals; another described § 54233 as covering HCD's review/enforcement role and prioritized negotiation periods. These characterizations are inconsistent.

6. **What "substantially prevail" encompasses.** One panelist (80) explicitly separated the legal-characterization question (very likely favoring HCD) from the remedy question (full invalidation not certain); the others treated the proposition as turning on the merits without drawing that distinction.

## 4. Authorities

**Cited by two or more panelists** (counts from formal authorities lists; one panelist provided no authorities list but referenced several of these sections in-line):

- Gov. Code § 54221(f) (any subsection variant) — 6
- Gov. Code § 54222 (Notice of Availability) — 6
- Gov. Code § 54230.5 (penalties/enforcement) — 6
- AB 1486 (Stats. 2019, ch. 664) — 6
- Gov. Code §§ 54220–54234 generally / § 54220 (findings/purpose) — 5
- AB 2135 — 3
- Gov. Code § 54223 (priority negotiation) — 2
- Gov. Code § 54233 — 2 (characterized differently; see Disagreement 5)
- AB 1255 — 2
- SB 6 — 2
- Health & Safety Code §§ 50079.5, 50093 (income-category definitions) — 2
- HCD Surplus Land Act Guidelines / technical-assistance materials — 2

**Cited by only one panelist:**

- Gov. Code § 54222.5
- Gov. Code § 54231
- Gov. Code § 54234 (charter-city applicability — flagged for verification)
- AB 480
- Health & Safety Code § 50105
- HCD published enforcement letters against other California cities (flagged as unverified)

Panelists are invited to review these themes and disagreements — particularly the divergent recollections of the exemption's percentage threshold, subsection location, and the defense/remedy questions — and submit revised round-2 estimates.

Independently reconsider. Do NOT defer to the majority; update only where the summary contains reasons you judge valid, and hold your position where it does not. Explain in response_to_panel which consideration moved you or why none did.

response_to_panel.description: which panel consideration moved you, or why none did

text: {"probability":80,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"HCD's position — that the parcel did not qualify as exempt surplus land and that the required NOA/priority-negotiation process was violated — would more likely than not substantially prevail. The deal's affordability structure (18/120 units, 15%, at a genuine lower-income restriction; 25-year covenants) sits well below any plausible reading of the negotiated-affordable-housing exemption's thresholds, whatever the exact current percentage/duration figures turn out to be. Independent of the exemption question, the city's undisputed failure to issue Notices of Availability under § 54222 before entering the ENA is a freestanding, easily provable procedural violation that survives regardless of how the exemption dispute resolves. I move from 78 to 80, converging toward the panel median, primarily because round-1 discussion showed that panelists reconstructing the exemption threshold from different, mutually inconsistent recollections nonetheless all independently concluded the deal fails — a robustness signal that reduces (without eliminating) my concern about misremembered statutory text. alone."],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether the current, amended text of Gov. Code § 54221(f) contains some exemption pathway (e.g., a mixed-income or transit-oriented development carve-out added by later legislation such as AB 2135 or 2023-2024 amendments) that could accommodate a 30%-affordable/25-year-covenant structure, and separately, whether a reviewing court would treat any Surplus Land Act violation as grounds for full invalidation/unwinding of the DDA versus a narrower remedy of penalties plus prospective compliance — the proposition's 'substantially prevail' language makes this remedy question potentially outcome-relevant, not merely academic.","changed":true,"response_to_panel":"What moved me from 78 to 80 was the robustness pattern in the disagreement section: panelists arrived at incompatible reconstructions of the exemption's exact percentage/duration threshold, yet every single one concluded the deal fails regardless of which reconstruction they used. That convergence-despite-disagreement is a meaningful (if imperfect) corroboration of the bottom-line legal characterization, and it also reinforced that the NOA violation (item C, 6/7 treating it as freestanding) gives HCD a second, independent path to prevail even if the exemption analysis is closer than any of us think. What did not move me further upward toward the 82-83 cluster: the shared self-flagged uncertainty (item I, 7/7) about exact codified thresholds is a limitation common to the whole panel's memory, not new verified information, so it can only partially offset my base-rate caution about citing unverified statutory specifics; and the remedy-narrowing counter-considerations (item H) raised by two lower-probability panelists strike me as legitimate open questions about what 'substantially prevail' means in practice, not fully answered by anything in the summary, so I did not adopt the higher-probability panelists' apparent view that remedy is not a live issue."}

data.position_summary: HCD's position — that the parcel did not qualify as exempt surplus land and that the required NOA/priority-negotiation process was violated — would more likely than not substantially prevail. The deal's affordability structure (18/120 units, 15%, at a genuine lower-income restriction; 25-year covenants) sits well below any plausible reading of the negotiated-affordable-housing exemption's thresholds, whatever the exact current percentage/duration figures turn out to be. Independent of the exemption question, the city's undisputed failure to issue Notices of Availability under § 54222 before entering the ENA is a freestanding, easily provable procedural violation that survives regardless of how the exemption dispute resolves. I move from 78 to 80, converging toward the panel median, primarily because round-1 discussion showed that panelists reconstructing the exemption threshold from different, mutually inconsistent recollections nonetheless all independently concluded the deal fails — a robustness signal that reduces (without eliminating) my concern about misremembered statutory text. I stop short of the panel's upper range (82–83) because remedy-scope questions (full DDA unwind vs. penalty-plus-prospective-compliance) and the residual possibility of an unrecalled alternative exemption pathway remain genuinely open on the facts given.
rationales: The affordable-housing exemption in Gov. Code § 54221(f) is narrow, and on every recalled variant of its threshold (near-100%-affordable framing, ~30% lower-income, ~25%+ lower-income), a project with only 15% genuine lower-income units and a 25-year covenant fails to qualify — the conclusion is robust across differing threshold assumptions, which is itself corroborating evidence for the legal characterization even though no panelist, myself included, can currently confirm the exact codified figures.

The city's undisputed failure to send Notices of Availability under § 54222 before entering the exclusive negotiating agreement is a freestanding, independently sufficient violation (as 6 of 7 panelists concluded) that does not depend on resolving the exemption dispute, which raises my confidence that some violation would be found even if the exemption question were closer than I estimate.

HCD's express statutory enforcement authority and penalty structure under § 54230.5, plus its post-AB 1486 track record of strict, textualist exemption readings, make it likely HCD's characterization would be sustained rather than treated as an overreaching or novel interpretation.

I did not move further upward (to the 82–83 range) because the proposition's 'held unlawful'/'substantially prevail' framing plausibly implicates remedy scope, and credible counter-considerations raised in round 1 (substantial-compliance, estoppel, laches, or a court narrowing the remedy to penalties-plus-prospective-compliance rather than unwinding the DDA) are not resolved by anything in the panel summary and remain a legitimate basis for some panelists' lower estimates.

The single most significant unresolved issue — shared by all seven round-1 panelists per their own self-flagged uncertainty — is that everyone is reconstructing the precise current statutory thresholds and exemption subsection from memory across multiple amendment cycles (AB 1486, AB 1255, SB 6, AB 2135, AB 480), and convergence among people relying on the same imperfect memory is weaker evidence than convergence among people independently verifying primary text, which caps how far I am willing to raise my estimate on that basis alone.
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether the current, amended text of Gov. Code § 54221(f) contains some exemption pathway (e.g., a mixed-income or transit-oriented development carve-out added by later legislation such as AB 2135 or 2023-2024 amendments) that could accommodate a 30%-affordable/25-year-covenant structure, and separately, whether a reviewing court would treat any Surplus Land Act violation as grounds for full invalidation/unwinding of the DDA versus a narrower remedy of penalties plus prospective compliance — the proposition's 'substantially prevail' language makes this remedy question potentially outcome-relevant, not merely academic.
data.response_to_panel: What moved me from 78 to 80 was the robustness pattern in the disagreement section: panelists arrived at incompatible reconstructions of the exemption's exact percentage/duration threshold, yet every single one concluded the deal fails regardless of which reconstruction they used. That convergence-despite-disagreement is a meaningful (if imperfect) corroboration of the bottom-line legal characterization, and it also reinforced that the NOA violation (item C, 6/7 treating it as freestanding) gives HCD a second, independent path to prevail even if the exemption analysis is closer than any of us think. What did not move me further upward toward the 82-83 cluster: the shared self-flagged uncertainty (item I, 7/7) about exact codified thresholds is a limitation common to the whole panel's memory, not new verified information, so it can only partially offset my base-rate caution about citing unverified statutory specifics; and the remedy-narrowing counter-considerations (item H) raised by two lower-probability panelists strike me as legitimate open questions about what 'substantially prevail' means in practice, not fully answered by anything in the summary, so I did not adopt the higher-probability panelists' apparent view that remedy is not a live issue.
